data.title: Apellis Pharmaceuticals: A Good Product That Just Misses The Mark
data.description:
Investment Overview - Apellis Stock Hits Painful Lows In First Half Of 2025

My last two ratings on Apellis Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (APLS), the Waltham, Massachusetts-based pharma company and complement system specialist, have been Holds, the first being in October 2023, and the more recent being in February 2024. In reality, Apellis stock is down 48% since my first note, and 63% since my second.

Let's remind ourselves of Apellis two commercial stage assets - as per the company's Q3 2025 10-Q filing:

We currently have two marketed drugs that target C3, the central protein in the complement cascade: SYFOVRE (pegcetacoplan injection), approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration, or FDA, in February 2023 for the treatment of geographic atrophy secondary to age-related macular degeneration, or GA; and EMPAVELI (pegcetacoplan), approved by the FDA in May 2021 for the treatment of paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria, or PNH and in July 2025 for the treatment of C3 glomerulopathy, or C3G, and primary immune complex membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis, or IC-MPGN.

With GA being the far larger indication, a few months post Syfovre's approval, Apellis stock was riding high, trading at >$90 per share in June 2023, then disaster struck. In July 2023, as I wrote in my last note, the:

American Society of Retinal Specialists ("ASRS") flagged several cases of eye inflammation and occlusive retinal vasculitis - a vision-threatening complication - in patients using Syfovre. Apellis stock price fell by >70% overnight, reaching a 5-year low value of ~$25 per share.

In the meantime, the Japanese Pharma giant Astellas (ALPMF) secured approval for a direct rival to Syfovre, Izervay, with a comparable safety and efficacy profile, in the same indication of GA - which had no approved therapies prior to the approval of Syfovre.

Apellis revealed in October 2023 that a total of ten cases of retinal vasculitis had been confirmed with two more suspected. Six patients recovered their vision, fully or partially, while three reportedly had severe vision impairment that was unlikely to be resolved. Apellis noted that the estimated real-world rate of retinal vasculitis was 0.01% per Syfovre injection.

The market pushed Apellis' share price back as high as ~$65, but stock has struggled since. A safety warnings was added to Syfovre's label in December 2023, and in January 2024, the European Medicines Agency ("EMA") declined to endorse Apellis' marketing authorization application ("MAA"), requesting permission to market and sell Syfovre in the EU. Apellis tried again, but in June 2024 the EMA once again issued a negative opinion, and another in September 2024.

In February this year, Apellis revealed 2024 revenues of $781.4m, up 97% year-on-year, with Syfovre earning $611.9m, and Empaveli $98.1m. The company reported a cash position of $411m, but with R&D expenses of $327.6m, and SG&A expenses of $501.1m, an annual net loss of $(197.9m) was reported, following on from a $(528.6m) net loss in 2023.

After Apellis reported Q1 2025 revenues of $166.8m, down from $172.3m in the prior year period (management put this down to "inventory and funding shortage at co-pay assistance programs"), cash position of $358.4m, and net loss of $(92.2m), compared to $(66.4m) in Q1 2024, stock sank to a five year low value of ~$16.5.

Apellis' Ups & Down In Second Half Of 2025

In late July this year, Apellis stock finally found some upside momentum after reporting the approval of injectable Emapaveli, according to a press release:

as the first treatment for C3 glomerulopathy (C3G) or primary immune complex membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis (IC-MPGN) in patients 12 years of age and older. C3G and primary IC-MPGN are rare kidney diseases, affecting 5,000 people in the United States.

In the Phase 3 VALIANT study, EMPAVELI demonstrated an unprecedented 68% reduction in proteinuria, stabilization of kidney function, and substantial clearance of C3 deposits as measured by C3 staining, compared to placebo.

Unfortunately, after climbing to a value of $30, stock nose-dived once again as Q3 earnings were reported at the end of October. Syfovre earned revenues of $151m, and Empaveli $27m - reflecting a strong launch in its new indications, according to management - while Apellis also earned $275m related to a royalty purchase arrangement agreed with its ex-U.S. partner Sobi related to sales of Aspaveli - the EU brand name for Emapaveli. As per its Q3 earnings press release:

Apellis and Sobi announced a capped royalty purchase agreement in July in which Apellis will receive up to $300 million in exchange for 90% of Apellis’ future ex-U.S. royalties for Aspaveli.

Cash position was reported as $479m, and year to date net income $81.3m, yet the market was clearly non-plussed, as stock sank in value, back <$20 per share. Presumably, flat sales of Syfovre, a slightly underwhelming launch in the new kidney disease indication, the sale of a key royalty stream to raise funds, and a lack of forward revenue guidance, were factors that contributed to the selloff.

Looking Ahead - Is There Fresh Hope For Apellis In 2026?

Astellas, the Japanese Pharma, has forecasted for ~$500m of Izervay revenues in 2025 (revising the figure down from ~$750m), which seemingly puts it on a par with Syfovre, although Apellis notes that:

SYFOVRE remains the market leader in GA with total market share exceeding an estimated 60% and 52% of new patient starts during the quarter.

Nevertheless, neither product seems close to fulfilling the multi-billion dollar peak revenue expectations analysts once set, and although Apellis finally reported a quarterly profit, without the royalty deal revenues, it seems that marketing and selling its key drug remains an unprofitable business.

Apellis reported 152 new start forms for Empaveli in its new indications since launch in Q3, which given its list price of ~$520k per annum, amounts to ~$76m, however, it is highly unlikely that Apellis earns the full list price, owing to rebates and discounts applied during negotiations with insurers, government, patients etc.

Management is targeting further kidney indications, - primary focal segmental glomerulosclerosis ("FSGS") and delayed graft function ("DGF"), with two pivotal studies expected to begin this year, and hopes to capture a greater share of the 1.5m patient GA market through awareness campaigns, as well as progressing a mid-stage study of pegcetacoplan plus candidate APL-3007, a small interfering RNA ("siRNA) drug candidate.

With shares currently trading ~$25, Apellis' market cap valuation today is $3.16bn. For me, this feels a little too high, for several reasons. Firstly, Syfovre, the supposed jewel in the crown of Apellis' business, is not growing revenues, not is it apparently a profitable drug to market and sell.

Worryingly, Astellas is struggling to drive >$500m revenues per annum for Izervay in the same indication, yet both drugs will enter their fourth year on the market in 2026. Throw in the safety setbacks and the refusal to approve the drug in the EU, and Syfovre does not feel like a "blockbuster" in waiting.

Secondly, in its new kidney disease indications, analysts have suggested peak revenues of ~$350m, which in my view, is not a high enough figure to persuade Wall Street to alter its valuation of Apellis' business, at least, not upwards. Although some analysts believe Syfovre will achieve >$1bn revenues per annum some time in the next decade, this strikes me as increasingly unlikely given performance in 2025.

Thirdly, Q3 was Apellis' first profitable quarter out of its last ten, but only because of the one-off Sobi deal, and cash position is >$500m. No guidance has been provided for 2025 or 2026, although management has said current cash is sufficient to last until profitability is achieved - but, its hard to see how profitability can be achieved based on current products and pipeline.

Concluding Thoughts

In summary, then, I am forced to award Apellis a reluctant Sell rating. I believe the company is being valued as if pegcetacoplan has the ability to generate >$1bn in peak revenues one day, and generate significant net profits for the company, but a pragmatic assessment of the current state of affairs at the company doesn't offer up many reasons to be optimistic that this will happen, in my view at least.

Eye disease markets are vast - witness the multibillion per annum revenue therapies such as Regeneron's (REGN) Eylea, or Roche's (RHHBY) Vabysmo - but only if you have the right product in the right market, and while I think Apellis has a strong drug product, with a well validated target in C3 - which management notes is "the only target in the complement cascade that addresses all 3 activation pathways that can initiate and drive disease" - I struggle to see how its captures sufficient quantities of market share in its various indications to achieve blockbuster status, and deliver net profits for Apellis in the long-term.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The First Bancorp: Attractive, But Keep An Eye On Loans Past Due
data.description:
Introduction

The First Bancorp, Inc. (FNLC) is the holding company of the First National Bank in Maine. The bank’s share price has been pretty flat since my previous article was published. Although the net income increased substantially, the total amount of loans past due also increased, so the focus will now shift towards managing the soured/souring loans.

The earnings: robust despite higher loan loss provisions

In the third quarter of the year, the company saw its total interest income increase by approximately 7% on a YoY basis while the total amount of interest expenses decreased by approximately 4% to just under $21M. The combination of both elements was an important driver of the net interest income, which increased by almost a quarter to exceed $20M.

As the income statement above shows, the bank also saw an increase in its non-interest income which came in handy to partially compensate the higher non-interest expenses recorded in the same quarter. This pushed the pre-tax and pre-provision income to $11.8M. And while loan loss provisions were elevated compared to the previous few quarters and definitely compared to the same quarter a year ago, when the bank was actually able to recoup some of its previously recorded provisions, the Q3 amount of provisioning remained relatively low in the greater scheme of things.

This resulted in a pre-tax profit of $11.1M and a net income of $9.1M. That’s about a fifth higher than in the same quarter a year ago, despite seeing a $1.34M swing in loan loss provisions from recovering previously recorded provisions to actual new loan loss provisions.

The EPS came in at $0.82 per share($0.81 on a diluted basis) in the third quarter, and this brought the 9M 2025 EPS to $2.19, representing an increase of almost 25% compared to the 9M 2024 EPS. And that once again included the impact of a swing in loan loss provisions going from a $0.64M recovery in 9M 2024 to a provision of almost $1.6M in the first nine months of the current financial year. The quarterly dividend of $0.37 per share remains well-covered.

An update on the loan book

Meanwhile, the bank’s balance sheet continues to grow, and the total amount of assets increased by approximately 1.5% at the end of Q3 compared to the same time a year ago. As you can see below, the balance sheet does not contain a whole lot of cash, but the bank has north of $600M in securities in a combination of securities available for sale and held to maturity. Keep in mind the fair value of the securities held to maturity is approximately $46M lower than the stated book value.

But my attention is, of course, drawn to the loan book. At the end of September 2025, the total size of the loan book increased to $2.4B, with approximately $25M in recorded loan loss allowances.

As you can see below, there’s little change in the breakdown of the loan book as the ratios remained relatively unchanged. The bank still predominantly is a lender to commercial-type borrowers with approximately 60% of its loan book geared towards commercial loans. Exceeding half of that, and about 33% of the entire loan book, is related to CRE, with a relatively even split between owner-occupied CRE and non-owner-occupied CRE.

It’s also still interesting to see that the bank continues to focus on its commercial and industrial loans while the total amount of construction loans has been cut in half.

I’m also glad to see the focus on residential loans remains strong as well, and I was, of course, curious to see the evolution in the total amount of loans past due.

It’s of course not unexpected to see an increase in the total amount of loans past due, due to the combination of an expanded loan book with a softening economy. However, there doesn’t appear to be a reason to be too alarmed just yet. As you can see below, the vast majority of the increase in loans past due is in the CRE segment, where the total amount of loans past due jumped from $0.55M at the end of FY 2024 to $5.5M at the end of September.

Fortunately, these loans are, of course, backed by real estate assets and it is unlikely that the entire loans will have to be written off. Depending on the type of asset and the LTV ratio, First Bancorp could perhaps avoid too much of a haircut on these loans, which does explain the relatively low amount of additional loan loss provisioning in the first nine months of the year. While the total amount of loans past due increased by $7.25M, the bank recorded just $1.6M in additional loan loss provisions.

Investment thesis

In my previous article, I mentioned I would keep an eye on the loan book and the total amount of loans past due. While it is not entirely unexpected to see the additional increase in loans past due, the situation likely shouldn’t get much worse before FNLC would have to increase its amount of quarterly loan loss provisions to ensure adequate coverage. I’m glad to see the vast majority of the loans past due are backed by real estate, and this should hopefully allow the bank to monetize the assets without taking too much of a haircut. And it goes without saying that lower interest rates on the financial markets should provide an improved climate for real estate transactions.

I currently still don’t have a long position in FNLC, but I will continue to keep an eye on this regional bank. I think a ‘buy’ is still an appropriate rating, mainly thanks to its strong earnings result despite the slightly higher loan loss provisions.

data.title: 2026 Market Outlook: A Grinding Bull Market In A Frothy S&P 500
data.description:
The SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY), which tracks the performance of the S&P 500, is up 17.59% year-to-date (YTD) in a year in which the Federal Reserve has cautiously resumed interest rate cutting, and the United States has embraced protectionist policies for the first time in decades as the average tariff rate rose from 2.50% to 17.9%, leading many to predict the end of globalization as we know it. Inflation, an ever-present topic since the COVID-19 pandemic, has continued to stalk the U.S. economy. This has also been a year in which the AI bubble has powered both the U.S. economy and S&P 500 returns. On September 27, Harvard economist Jason Furman estimated that without the data center buildout, U.S. gross domestic product (GDP) growth on an annualized basis would have been an anemic 0.1%. As 2025 comes to an end, these factors -interest rates, the impact of the AI bubble, inflation, and the prospect of a recession-, will prove decisive in determining the level of the S&P 500 in 2026. The procedure taken in this forecast is Bayesian, so, first, I will establish a base rate, and then, look at the most likely drivers of returns and adjust the base rate up or down in response. With a projected 6.8% return, I maintain a Buy rating on the SPY for 2026, albeit with expectations for lower returns and higher volatility than recent years.

The Base Rate: Wall Street Expects a 9.08% Gain on Current Levels

My approach to forecasting is Bayesian, so the first port-of-call will be to establish a base rate, which I will do by reviewing what I consider the most important outlooks from asset managers, independent macro shops, large universal banks, and sell-side strategists. This is similar to how I would predict the gold price, which can be tracked on Gold Eagle Coin, and then use that to anchor our expectations. This means 2026 will either be far better or far worse than consensus estimates. My review shows that these institutions remain bullish over the S&P 500’s prospects. The table below shows their target prices and the implied performance compared to the S&P 500’s current level of 6,948.91.

From these forecasts, we get an equal-weighted consensus target level of 7,579.69, for an upside of 9.08%, above the 8.1% historical average. This will be my base rate and will anchor my forecast.

It is clear that JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM), HSBC Holdings plc (HSBC), and UBS Group AG (UBS) converge exactly at 7,500, which is unlikely to be independent model convergence and more likely reflects anchoring on a round-number multiple, shared earnings assumptions, and institutional herding behavior. Overall, there is a clear optimism bias, with Oppenheimer Asset Management, Deutsche Bank (DB), Morgan Stanley (MS), RBC Capital Markets, Capital Economics, and Ed Yardeni having the most bullish calls. Driving the overall optimism is the belief that the benefits of AI spending will lead to earnings growth, while the resilience of the U.S. economy will shelter the S&P 500 and reduced policy uncertainty will embolden managers. Wells Fargo & Company (WFC) and the Bank of America (BAC) stand out for their conservative forecasts of levels between 7,100 and 7,200.

The Bank of America, on the other hand, breaks from the pack, with its most startling argument being that there is an “AI air pocket” driving returns despite hyperscalers becoming more asset-heavy and taking on more debt, so that "de-rating may be warranted in the months ahead". Wells Fargo’s caution is largely illusory, being a product of their outlook being released in September, when they set a 6,650 target for the S&P 500 for the end of this year. When they published their outlook, Ohsung Kwon, an analyst at Wells Fargo, stated that

“With the S&P 500 trading at 22x, we expect future returns to be more driven by EPS than multiples. In fact, the market has become increasingly earnings-driven. Over the past 12 months, 2/3 of the S&P 500's return has been driven by forward EPS growth, a stark contrast to the multiple-led market in 2022-23”.

Moreover, acknowledging that continued rally depends on a single factor, Kwon added that “...as long as AI capex remains intact, the bull market should continue”.

Uncertainty

Forecasts are typically wrong: an analysis of such forecasts reveals that between 2020 and 2024, the Bank of Montreal (BMO) had the most accurate forecasts, and they had an average absolute error of around 10%, while the average firm had an average error of 15%! Throughout this period, the average return of the S&P 500 was 6.2%, so the typical forecast was off by more than twice the actual return. A similar analysis by Investopedia found that analysts are often way off.

In the table above, consensus estimates of an average 6.36% return between 2018 and 2025 led to an average actual return of 7.36%. On average, the difference is small, but year-to-year, it is quite large. What we see is that analysts tend to be directionally correct but overly conservative. Analysts systematically underestimate the range of outcomes, and in late-cycle, high-valuation environments, that error distribution becomes negatively skewed because downside surprises dominate once upside optionality is exhausted.

Bayesian Adjustment: -1%.

The Long View: A Frothy Market

According to data from investment analyst Joseph Noko, U.S. equity preference, a measure of the allocation to equity in the aggregate portfolio, is 53.27% as of Q2 2025, its highest level ever. U.S. equity preference explains 74.5% of subsequent S&P 500 returns. There is an inverse relationship between U.S. equity preference and subsequent 10-year returns, with the current level implying average 10-year returns of about 1%.

Similarly, measures such as the Cyclically Adjusted PE Ratio (CAPE Ratio) the ratio of corporate equities to GDP -known as the “Buffett Indicator” because it is favored by Warren Buffett of Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A)- are at some of their highest levels ever. That said, long-range forecasts are very poor at forecasting returns over the next year, often leaving money on the table.

While these factors do not predict an imminent crash, they do imply downside skew, compressed upside, and an elevated probability that consensus overshoots.

Bayesian Adjustment: -2.5%

The Midterms Loom

According to a 2023 report by the U.S. Bancorp (USB), midterm elections for the U.S. Senate and House of Representatives have resulted in the S&P 500 underperforming in the year leading up to them, with average returns of 0.3%, compared to the 8.1% historical average.

Although policy uncertainty in the run-up to the midterms sounds like a plausible reason for the S&P 500 to perform, there are two reasons to rule this out as an explanation. First, the stock market is coming off one of the most uncertain years in U.S. history in terms of policy, and signs of a Democratic victory would probably boost the market because that would signal a return to the status quo, whereas signs of a Republican victory would merely confirm that the current policy regime is here to stay. Second, U.S. Bancorp’s analysis found that “the outcome of midterm elections has no noticeable impact on overall equity market performance.” I would add the proviso that, typically, Republicans and Democrats are fairly close in terms of overall policy, differing on the margins, whereas the changes brought about by the Trump Administration, at least regarding tariffs, are truly a break from anything the U.S. has done over the last 80 years or so. That, however, is answered by the first point. Nevertheless, U.S. Bancorp’s analysis finds that, historically, the outcome of midterm elections reflects the state of the economy. They explain that,

“The last time the S&P 500 Index produced negative returns during the 12 months after a midterm election was 1939—a time of tremendous economic contraction and uncertainty as the U.S. battled the Great Depression and World War II began in Europe. This also explains why negative pre-midterm market returns dominated the 1960s and 1970s, pulling down the overall pre-midterm average. The late 1960s and 1970s were a time of slow economic growth marked by high unemployment, rising energy prices and significant inflation. If you exclude the five midterm elections in the 1960s and 1970s, the average S&P return for pre-midterm election years is 8.1%—roughly in line with average annual S&P 500 performance. Since then, the economy has grown steadily, with accommodating central bank policy keeping inflation low. This suggests that a healthy overall macroeconomic environment carries greater weight than any policy uncertainty.”

As discussed in the introduction, absent AI spending, U.S. economic activity would have grown by just 0.1% for the year ended in September 2025. A recent JPMorgan Chase analysis estimated that AI-related capital expenditures contributed 1.1% to GDP growth, “outpacing the U.S. consumer as an engine of expansion.”

Bayesian Adjustment: +0.00%

The OBBBA Will Boost Capital Expenditures

The One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) permanently reinstated 100% bonus depreciation in Internal Revenue Code §168(k), allowing firms to fully depreciate qualified property bought and brought into service after January 19, 2025. This provision comes into effect in 2026 and could lead to even more capital expenditure beyond AI. Economists generally believe that bonus depreciation lowers the effective tax cost of capital, which can promote investment. The Budget Lab at Yale found that 100% bonus depreciation increases real investment in eligible assets by roughly 10 to 18% relative to assets not eligible for the tax break. These results align with standard economic theory: lowering the user cost of capital stimulates firms to invest more because the after-tax return on new capital rises. 100% bonus depreciation will also raise long-run GDP and accelerate the payback on investments.

Bayesian Adjustment: +0.5%

The U.S. Will Avoid a Recession

JPMorgan Chase sees AI as a new bellwether of growth. While observing that not all AI spending will translate into GDP growth, they argue that it is shoring up the economy “at a time when consumption is softening and rates remain elevated and shows some independence to variables like interest rates, labor markets, and even trade shocks.” However, it is easy to overstate the case.

Recession dynamics are observed by events in the labor market, not in GDP growth. The most credible indicator of recession, the SAHM Rule, indicates that a recession has begun when the three-month moving average of the national unemployment rate rises by 0.50 percentage points or more relative to the minimum of the three-month averages from the previous 12 months.

As Claudia Sahm, the economist who developed the indicator, explained in a recent Substack,

Job creation (top left) has slowed to a crawl, but the unemployment rate (top right) has only edged up over the past year, suggesting that a softening demand for labor has been largely offset by a softening supply of labor.

Most importantly, she said,

“I do not see recessionary dynamics in the labor market now”, given the current labor dynamics.

In that vein, it is interesting to note that Nouriel Roubini, often called, “Dr. Doom”, suggested that the U.S. is heading into what he called a “growth recession (meaning below-trend GDP growth) for a few months, followed by a recovery and a gradual decline in the inflation rate toward the US Federal Reserve’s 2% target. Think of this as the Goldilocks scenario.” He went on to explain that,

“The Goldilocks scenario is the baseline because market discipline, good advisers, and a still-independent Fed (notwithstanding Trump’s periodic threats) have already forced the White House to blink and climb down from the high tariffs announced on April 2. Since then, the administration has negotiated various trade deals and frameworks featuring more modest tariff increases (often in exchange for commitments to invest in the United States). As a result, US and global growth have slowed somewhat, but inflation has risen only modestly.”

This, I think, is reasonable given the resilience shown in 2025.

Bayesian Adjustment: +0.25%

Monetary Easing Will Boost Valuations

The appointment of a new Federal Reserve Chair, likely Kevin Hassett, will signal a more vigorous round of monetary easing. Standard economic theory says that if interest rates are lowered, then the value of future cash flows increases. Consequently, the monetary easing demanded by the president, and which his nominee will share, will boost S&P 500 valuations.

Bayesian Adjustment: +0.25%

Massive Asset Write-Downs Are Unlikely in 2026

Since the start of the AI bubble, Big Tech has largely beaten earnings expectations, despite gloomy foreshadowing. For example, Alphabet (GOOGL) looks stronger than ever and now appears the biggest challenger to OpenAI’s dominance in consumer LLMs; NVIDIA (NVDA) continues to surprise with bigger earnings than expected; and even Meta Platforms (META), whose Llama has lagged behind ChatGPT, Gemini, and Claude, has been incredibly profitable. There are no signs of the kind of revenue and earnings slowdown that suggests that the AI bubble is over. What is more likely is that asset write-downs will trigger a sell-off. However, this is unlikely in 2026. Dario Perkins of Lombard wrote in a note that

“Leveraged bubbles (which typically involve property markets) are far deadlier than unleveraged bubbles (in stock markets, tulips, Bitcoin etc). The reason is simple. Falling asset prices will damage balance sheets, and if debt (which is fixed in nominal terms) has increased a lot, that can trigger a painful dynamic of asset fire sales and forced deleveraging . . . there is not (yet) a lot of leverage in the current AI capex bubble.”

Although firms like Meta have entered into private debt investment deals, they tend to be relatively small. It is true that Oracle Corporation (ORCL) has grown total debt from $108.96 billion to $124.39 billion in just one year and now has the financial profile of Antofagasta plc, a Chilean copper miner, and nine American utilities. However, while AI has certainly turned Big Tech into more industrial firms, we have not yet entered the point at which their debt profiles are broadly excessive.

Bayesian Adjustment: +0.25%

Outlook: Prepare for 6.83% Returns

Adjusting for all these factors leads to a target return of 6.83 from current levels and a target level of 7,423.52. This leads to a “Buy” rating, but with lower expected returns and higher volatility.

The central tension in this outlook is between historically extreme valuation signals and unusually strong cyclical support, and the most coherent resolution is not a crash or a melt-up, but a period of positive yet compressed returns. It is no wonder that so many institutions crowded around a target of 7,500. Record U.S. equity preference and elevated CAPE ratios do not predict imminent drawdowns; rather, they indicate that future returns will be increasingly dependent on realized earnings growth rather than valuation expansion. At the same time, AI-related capital expenditure -reinforced by permanent 100% bonus depreciation- has become a partial substitute for traditional growth engines, stabilizing aggregate demand even as consumption softens. In such an environment, the S&P 500 can plausibly grind higher into 2026, but upside will be capped by starting valuations and concentration risk, leaving the market vulnerable to episodic volatility while avoiding the balance-sheet stress that characterizes more destructive bubbles.

data.title: CNX Resources: Not Much Has Changed
data.description:
CNX Resources (CNX) is a management that I will never forget. Back around 2014, CNX Midstream went public with the midstream assets of CNX. Starting in fiscal year 2020, in my last article (and articles before that) I wrote that things were pointing towards financial stress in the parent company, CNX Resources. Not long after that, CNX Midstream experienced a take-under by parent company CNX Resources after a dividend cut. In the roughly 5-plus years that the company was public, any shareholder that held from the going public price suffered a loss of most of the principal while collecting midstream-style dividends. CNX shareholders made out just fine in the situation, while CNX Midstream shareholders lost a fair amount of money in the time the company was public. Managements that do something like that are, for me, a long-term avoid.

Long-Term Debt

What is more interesting is that after a recent acquisition, the net debt is almost exactly the same as it was back in fiscal year 2020.

When it comes to material items that have not changed in the five years (roughly) that I last wrote about the company, net debt is way up the list. This is what caused a lot of issues last time around.

In the same company presentation, the net debt ratio is in the 2.0 area, which is also too high. In comparison, both Antero Resources (AR) and EQT Corporation (EQT) have achieved investment grade debt in roughly the same time frame. That is what should have happened here.

This is supposedly a low-cost producer that can generate free cash flow.

This fiscal year, free cash flow has been augmented by asset sales. Yet an acquisition was made that returned debt to the level it was when I wrote my last article. Furthermore, the level of free cash flow this fiscal year (asset sales and all) is still at a level that would require a few years to repay the outstanding debt.

In an industry with volatile commodity prices where the future has low visibility, this is a strategy that is asking for trouble. This stock is definitely not for risk averse or conservative investors.

Now, admittedly, natural gas prices are supposed to improve as the supply tightens. But this management appears to prefer to retire stock rather than get the debt level down "just in case". That makes this one very risky idea.

For the record, there appears to be enough cash flow to handle the debt coming due in fiscal year 2026. If something were to happen that the debt cannot be repaid, then the bank line should at least be able to temporarily carry that debt. That does allow some financial flexibility. So, there are no immediate problems on the horizon.

Stock Price Action

The stock price appears to have roughly quadrupled from its fiscal year 2020 low to the present time.

While this is not a bad return, others did the same or better while reducing debt considerably. A high debt ratio can hinder stock price valuation in the current market atmosphere.

This data from the website demonstrates that the leverage that CNX has is really not doing it any good when compared to other Marcellus producers. Those other Marcellus producers went on to reduce debt and improve corporate metrics to achieve investment grade. Yet CNX has made no debt progress, and the return shown above in roughly the same time period really shows that shareholders could have done better with ideas that (went on to have) ended up with more highly rated debt.

Investing is about avoiding unnecessary risk. Here there is financial risk that can clearly be avoided elsewhere. In an industry like this, there is really no benefit going forward to that leverage if the past is any guide.

Summary

For me, CNX is too risky to consider. The debt ratio remains too high. That is an extra "bonus" to combine with the fact that I do not trust management after what happened with CNX Midstream back in fiscal year 2020.

CNX has always been a low-cost producer, as shown above. But keep in mind that we are talking about essentially a dry gas producer where the liquids production is so low that essentially it does not do much for the average price received. This low-cost producer should benefit materially from the anticipated strengthening of natural gas prices.

However, the treatment years ago of CNX Midstream shareholders that forced the midstream to make a sizable payment to the parent company was followed by a dividend cut that cratered the stock price. Then the resulting takeover offer was a foregone conclusion.

But this is the kind of thing that makes me wonder what management might do in the future. Admittedly, CNX Resources at the time had cash flow issues compared to the outstanding debt from a few years of natural gas price declines. How it was handled was definitely in the interests of CNX Resources shareholders.

However, I just think that investors can do better than what is going on here. The current debt ratio as shown above is still much too high even for a low-cost natural gas producer. Sometimes, things just do not change.

Admittedly, the debt level rose in the current fiscal year due to an acquisition. But the debt repayment progress was very slow in the first place (for that to happen). That slow pace was due to a management decision to retire stock rather than repay debt.

As someone who avoids risk (and there seems to be evidence of plenty here), I would rate this a very strong sell. It is not so much that I believe that the stock price will go down as I believe there are much better opportunities elsewhere with management that can be trusted.

Risk

What is needed here is a major change in strategy by management towards a more conservative balance sheet. This is a company with low enough costs that it should easily achieve investment-grade status if it tried for such a goal. Two competitors, Antero Resources and EQT have both achieved investment grade status for their debt.

The other thing would be that something like what happened with CNX Midstream would assuredly not happen again in any form. But the relatively high debt ratio almost assures another "crisis situation" that management would have to climb out of.

The high debt ratio also means that there is some exposure during a cyclical downturn to being unable to properly service the debt load. Now admittedly, this company has one of the lowest costs in the industry. But it also has no significant value-added products as part of the production. The liquids production is too small a percentage to materially change the average price received per MCF or BOED. That leaves this company open to a natural gas price downturn, whereas some competitors have liquids production to buffer any severe or sustained situation.

The company will benefit from the expected tightening of the natural gas supply that should result in stronger prices. But future prices are both volatile and low visibility. A severe and sustained downturn can be particularly harmful to a company with an above-average debt ratio.

data.title: Global X 2026 Outlook - 10 In EM: Back To Basics
data.description:
International Allocations

Emerging Markets (EM) appear poised to enter 2026 with the right mix of ingredients for a potentially positive cycle.

These include a weaker or steady U.S. dollar, increased liquidity in the financial system, strength in China, and low investor positioning in EM securities. These factors drove the last EM upswing, when the MSCI Emerging Markets Index outperformed the S&P 500 Index 344.4% to 15.07% (cumulative) between January 1, 2001, and December 31, 2010.[1] We believe those conditions could again be supportive for EM now. Against this backdrop, we aim to balance cyclical and structural growth stories into the new year with a focus on valuations and profitability versus cost of capital. Our highest-conviction ideas take investors back to BASICs, as we see potential opportunities in Brazil, Argentina, Southeast Asia, India, and China (specifically within consumer), among other themes and regions.

Key EM Opportunities

What's Moving the Needle?

U.S. Dollar

The dollar fell roughly 8% in the first 10 months of 2025, as measured by the U.S. Dollar Index (DXY).[2] A dovish Federal Reserve, widening growth differentials favoring international markets, and public acknowledgment from Washington that a weaker dollar supports trade all added downward pressure.

Liquidity

The Fed’s renewed easing cycle has injected liquidity back into the system. Over the past 25 years, EM equities have outperformed the S&P 500 Index by an average of 11.9% and 15.4% during the 6- and 12-month periods following the final cut of Fed rate easing cycles.[3] EM dollar-denominated bonds have also exceeded U.S. Investment-Grade bonds by 4.6% and 8.8% in those same windows.[4]

China

The World Bank has upgraded its Chinese GDP growth expectations from 4% to 4.8%.[5] The October meeting between Presidents Trump and Xi marked an important step towards resolving bilateral tensions and fostering economic progress.

Positioning

From a positioning perspective, most advisors we speak to still have, on average, less than 5% exposure to EM equities. However, 2025 was the first time in many years that we heard noticeable concerns about underweights to the asset class and a desire to get closer to the 10.5% benchmark weight in the MSCI All Country World Index.

Icing On The Cake?

In addition to these high-level macroeconomic drivers, we acknowledge that politics can also play a key role in EM investing. In Latin America, the 2025-2026 period brings various elections, and we have already seen the political pendulum begin to swing from the extreme left back to the center-right. India should continue to thrive under Prime Minister Modi and reforms initiated by the Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP). Greece’s New Democracy Party continues to focus on both debt reduction and growth. Even the Chinese Communist Party (CCP) has stepped in the direction of neighboring Japan and Korea in terms of market-oriented reforms.

With these factors in place, we believe 2025 could be the first inning of a multi-year uptrend for Emerging Market assets.

Despite our bullish outlook, we acknowledge that broad EM index exposure isn’t for everyone and highlight some regional and country-specific options to gain exposure to the growth opportunities and attractive valuations we see across Emerging Markets.

1 - Argentina - High Flying, Adored

Monitoring

Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) Acceleration

Commodity and infrastructure projects should attract new foreign investment.

Foreign Exchange (FX) Flexibility

Authorities are likely to continue easing currency controls, eventually reaching a free float.

More Tax & Labor Reforms

Congress may fast-track a reform package simplifying the tax code and modernizing labor rules to boost formal employment.

Pension & Fiscal Consolidation

Targeted spending cuts should anchor a medium-term fiscal adjustment, reinforcing International Monetary Fund (IMF) support.

MSCI Index Inclusion

As liquidity improves and foreign access expands, Argentina could requalify for MSCI Emerging Markets status, potentially unlocking a new wave of institutional flows.

We believe Argentina’s equity market now offers one of the most asymmetric opportunities in Emerging Markets. Credible policy, cheap valuations, and rising institutional engagement position it for a potential multi-year rerating.

President Javier Milei’s October 2025 decisive midterm victory reinforced investor confidence and expanded his ability to deliver market-friendly structural reforms. Equities recently traded at roughly 8.5x forward earnings, well below regional peers, potentially leaving room for multiple expansion as fundamentals strengthen.[6] We see parallels to Brazil in 1995: inflation is cooling, reforms are gaining traction, and markets are awakening after years of dysfunction.

Milei has done much of the “heavy lifting” in his first two years in office, and his efforts have delivered results (higher growth, lower inflation, lower poverty).

However, there are plenty of opportunities for improvement.

2 - Colombia - The Next Argentina?

Monitoring

Congressional Elections

March 8, 2026.

Presidential Elections

May 31st and June 21st, 2026, for a run-off.

Energy Prices

Oil and oil derivatives represent Colombia’s number one export.[9]

Venezuela

Potential political change.

Staying in Latin America with a focus on political change, Colombian equities may present one of the most compelling contrarian opportunities in the region. Valuations remain deeply discounted, reflecting years of policy uncertainty and perceived mismanagement, yet the upcoming May Presidential election could mark a turning point. A market-friendly political shift could unlock investment, stabilize fiscal policy, and reignite growth. Moreover, Colombia stands to benefit indirectly from potential regime change in Venezuela, with improved trade, migration normalization, and regional sentiment offering tailwinds. In our view, Colombia is priced for stagnation but positioned for recovery, creating an attractive setup for investors seeking potential asymmetric upside.

3 - Brazil - Two Catalysts Align

Monitoring

The Central Bank

We expect the COPOM to begin cutting rates in Q1 of 2026.

Tarcisio de Freitas

The Governor of Sao Paulo needs to announce if he is running for president by April of 2026.

Presidential Elections

First round on October 4, 2026. Run-off would be on October 25th.

Brazil goes into 2026 with two key performance drivers: interest rates and politics. In terms of monetary policy, Brazil’s key rate is poised to end 2025 at 15%. With inflation around 5%, that translates into one of the highest real rates in the world, which should support a powerful carry trade and buoy the Brazilian real (BRL).[10] We expect rate cuts beginning in the first quarter of 2026, which would likely act as a powerful standalone force for Brazilian equities. The prevalence of floating-rate debt in Brazil means lower interest rates should reduce net interest expenses for corporations and lift earnings (before we see other positive effects in the form of improved asset quality, more loan growth, more capex, and growth!). Looking at the past seven completed rate cutting cycles in Brazil, the average MSCI Brazil Index (Net) rally stood at roughly +95% (cumulative), where the average drawdown was around -15% (cumulative).[11] We like this risk-reward set-up.

Looking further out, Brazil hosts Presidential elections in October of 2026. The incumbent is expected to run for his fourth term in office and, if elected, would be 81 by the start of his new term. He has recently faced multiple health challenges and a high rejection rate. Noticing a pattern of Senate and Congressional elections moving towards the center-right, we believe that there is a legitimate opportunity for a market-friendly, fiscally responsible center-right candidate to step into the presidency.

Avg MSCI Brazil Performance During Completed Cutting Cycles

Rallies

96.7%

Drawdowns

-14.5%

Source: Bloomberg and Global X data September 30, 2025

4 - India - A Standalone Growth Opportunity

Monitoring

Trade

We expect continued progress on trade talks with the U.S. along with implementation of the new free trade agreement with the EU.

Consumption

We expect an uptick in consumer data as the government’s recent stimulus filters through the economy.

Foreign Direct Investment

We expect the “Make in India” program to continue driving multinational investment into India.

India lagged the rest of EM through the first 10 months of 2025 due to trade uncertainty, but we believe that various near-term factors could support equity market performance. On the policy front, a dovish Reserve Bank of India (RBI) and recent consumer-friendly fiscal reforms could lend support. Commodities are another potential tailwind in the form of low energy prices (India imports ~90% of its energy needs) and high gold prices (~20% of household savings lie in gold).[12, 13] Any profit taking from China’s recent rally could also be a catalyst, as could steps towards a U.S./India trade deal. Longer term, we see India offering compelling long-term growth potential and serving as an enticing standalone investment.

5 - Southeast Asia - Diversified Value and Growth

ASEAN (Association of Southeast Asian Nation) equities offer a compelling blend of diversification and structural growth potential, supported by favorable demographics and policy stability. The region stands out as a key beneficiary of global supply chain diversification away from China, with Indonesia and Malaysia expanding semiconductor capacity and Vietnam, Malaysia, and Thailand emerging as alternative sources for rare earth elements. This shift is attracting record FDI inflows and deepening ASEAN’s role in global manufacturing. Combined with diverse economic drivers, from energy in Malaysia to tourism in the Philippines and Thailand, the bloc provides investors with multi-sector exposure to one of the world’s most compelling long-term growth stories.

Monitoring

“Friendshoring”

We expect more Western companies to diversify supply chains out of China and across SE Asia.

Rare Earths

We are keeping an eye on potential policy shifts and trade agreements in the critical mineral space.

Semiconductors

We are monitoring government incentives and foreign direct investment into semiconductor manufacturing.

6 - Vietnam - Good Morning, Vietnam!

Monitoring

Index Inclusion

Following the announcement from FTSE, we are monitoring language for potential inclusion of Vietnam into the MSCI Emerging Markets Index.

"China +1"

We expect capacity expansion in electronics, semiconductors, and other high value manufacturing segments as both Chinese and Western companies diversify production into the country.

Reforms

We look for the continuation of deregulation and the government’s shrinking role in the economy.

We think Vietnam is entering 2026 positioned to accelerate. The nation is benefitting from global supply chain diversification and the “China+1” narrative, contributing to the government’s target of 10% (!) annual GDP growth in 2026.[14] Finally, the recent FTSE Russell upgrade from Frontier to Emerging Market status unlocks billions in passive flows, and we believe an MSCI upgrade should follow.

7 - China - Assessing the Positives and Negatives

China is a large, complex, and dynamic economy. We unpack our key areas of focus for 2026 and their potential implications on markets:

POSITIVE NEGATIVE

Trade

Recent progress on trade negotiations should boost equity market sentiment. However, exports now contribute only ~2% to GDP growth.[15] China has also diversified its demand base. Less than 15% of exports went to the U.S. in 2024, down from 21% in 2002.[16] The real priority for Beijing is easing U.S. tech export controls, not boosting goods trade.

Taiwan

Tensions persist, but Beijing’s strategic calculus remains pragmatic: it seeks stability ahead of any U.S. policy shifts under Trump, avoiding conflict that could disrupt vital imports like semiconductors, LNG, and iron ore.

Technology

President Xi’s agenda centers on technological self-reliance. R&D spending has surged 251% since 2012, with breakthroughs in robotics, EVs, and green tech.[17] Beijing aims to reduce dependence on Western inputs while leveraging its near-monopoly in rare earth processing as strategic leverage in future negotiations.[18]

Demographics

With a shrinking labor force, China is countering demographic decline through automation and education investment. Over half of global industrial robots are now installed in China, and domestic firms lead production, elevating productivity even as the population ages. China’s robot density ranked third globally in 2023, at 470 units per 10,000 manufacturing employees, well ahead of the U.S. (295).[20]

Rare Earths

“The Middle East has oil. China has rare earths.” Deng Xiaoping, 1992. China dominates global refining of critical minerals and produces 92% of global rare earth magnets, which are essential components in many U.S. weapons, among other key goods.[19]

Capital Markets

China is showing early signs of pursuing governance reforms and shareholder-friendly initiatives similar to the “value-up” programs seen in Korea and Japan. Regulators are encouraging higher dividend payouts, buybacks, and improved transparency, potentially unlocking value in a deeply discounted equity market trading well below historical averages.

Consumer!

The consumer discretionary sector aligns with Beijing’s long-term policy objectives around “common prosperity” and doubling the middle class by 2035. Meanwhile, China’s pivot toward value-added technology supports innovation-driven growth. The sector spans tech, internet, autos, travel, hospitality, and education, offering investors diversified exposure to the rising purchasing power and policy-backed transformation of the Chinese economy.

Monitoring

Technology

China’s focus on self-sufficiency in semiconductors, AI, and green tech will likely intensify in 2026 as it seeks to offset export controls and sustain innovation-led growth.

Consumption Stimulus

The CCP continues to emphasize growing domestic consumption, but we are yet to see the necessary stimulus or social safety nets to move the needle.

Geopolitics

Potential reemergence of U.S.–China tensions and supply-chain realignments will keep external risks elevated.

8 - Greece - Greece is Good

Monitoring

Index inclusion

Following the announcement from FTSE, we are watching language for potential inclusion of Greece into the MSCI Developed Markets Index.

M&A

We are monitoring headlines around potential targets in the Greek financial space from Western European peers.

Greek equities have shown remarkable momentum of late, with the MSCI Greece Index (Net) climbing over 70% year-to-date through October 31, 2025.[21] Sovereign upgrades to Investment-Grade status by all three major rating agencies came from a result of responsible governance, fiscal discipline, and EU backing. Similarly, FTSE has already announced the upgrade of Greece to Developed Market status. MSCI could be next, which would likely provide a catalyst by unlocking passive inflows. Aided by Recovery and Resilience Facility (RRF) fund inflows and tourism, Greek GDP is projected to expand 2.2% in 2026, ahead of Eurozone peers.[22] Despite all of these positives and strong fundamentals, the MSCI Greece Index recently traded at a price-to-earnings ratio of 8.7x and a price-to-book ratio of 1.2x, well below the S&P 500 Index.[23] Overall, we believe that Greek equities offer EM-style growth and valuations with DM-level stability, yet they are still underowned and poised for a potential re-rating.

9 - EM Bonds - Potential Yield and Diversification Benefits

Emerging Market fixed income enters 2026 on strong footing. Disinflation, resilient growth, and Fed easing support potential flows and performance. In addition to yield and diversification benefits, we also see opportunities for growth as we note improved fiscal discipline in key countries. Overall, we believe EM debt can provide compelling risk-adjusted income as global rates normalize and risk appetite improves.

Monitoring

Monetary Policy

EM dollar-denominated bonds have historically rallied and outperformed U.S. Investment-Grade bonds in the six- and twelve-month periods following the final Fed rate cut.

The USD

Weakness in the U.S. dollar should support EM dollar-denominated bond performance as it lowers external debt burdens and boosts commodity-linked revenues supporting credit conditions.

Positioning

EM debt is often underrepresented in both global bond indices and institutional portfolios.

10 - Commodities - Building Blocks of EM Performance

Commodities and Emerging Markets have long moved in lockstep, not only because EM countries tend to be both the largest producers and consumers of raw materials, but because they are both subject to fluctuations in the U.S. dollar. Looking into the start of 2026, we expect to see improving global GDP growth, especially in more cyclical sectors, to drive more economically sensitive commodities higher while we believe we remain in a structural bull market in precious metals. We also continue to see medium-term supply deficits building in key areas such as copper, lithium, and energy.

Monitoring

Precious Metals

Gold stands to benefit from a weaker USD and continued central bank buying, while rising gold prices act as a powerful tailwind for EM consumer balance sheets, especially those in India and China. Higher Gold prices should benefit producers, as well. Silver carries similar traits to gold but should prove more economically sensitive to cyclical recoveries.

Copper

Copper would likely be a key beneficiary of any cyclical recovery, while robust structural demand (electrification, urbanization, and artificial intelligence) and a challenged supply outlook (declining grades, aging mines, and rising jurisdictional risk) should support prices throughout the cycle.

Energy

Oil prices remain subject to changes in OPEC+ supply quotas in the short term, while the nuclear power revival and challenged supply support Uranium market fundamentals.

In Conclusion

In finding a broad and balanced allocation, we like the idea of balancing value and growth along with economies that benefit from both cyclical and structural trends.

B

BRAZIL

High real rates create an attractive carry trade and margin expansion setup. The next rate-cutting cycle could ignite a broader equity re-rating as politics trend more market-friendly ahead of the 2026 election.
A

ARGENTINA

Reform momentum, credible policy execution, and attractive valuations point to one of EM’s most asymmetric opportunities as FX flexibility, FDI inflows, and potential MSCI re-inclusion drive re-engagement.
S

SOUTHEAST ASIA

The region plays a significant role in global supply chain diversification, with rising FDI in semiconductors, energy, and logistics supporting ongoing growth and policy stability.
I

INDIA

A dovish RBI, consumer-focused fiscal policy, and low energy prices support near-term upside, while structural reforms and digital innovation support long-term compounding.
C

CHINA CONSUMER

Post–Trump/Xi talks, trade friction risk eases as Beijing prioritizes tech self-reliance, automation, and governance reforms, laying the groundwork for potential flows to consumer and innovation leaders.

Appendix

  3. Bloomberg LP. Average returns based off five cutting cycles: 1/3/2001 to 12/11/2001; 11/6/2002 to 6/25/2003; 9/18/2007 to 12/16/2008; 8/1/2019 to 10/31/2019; and 3/3/2020 to 3/16/2020.
  4. Ibid.

  6. Bloomberg LP. Data as of November 13, 2025.
  7. Reuters. (2024, Jul 16). Argentina Posts Six Months of Fiscal, Financial Surpluses.

  12. The New Indian Express. (2025, Apr 18). Crude oil Import Up by 4.2% to 242 MT in FY25.

  14. Reuters. (2025, Oct 20). Vietnam Targets 10% GDP Growth in 2026 Despite External Pressures.

Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Diversification does not ensure a profit nor guarantee against a loss.

The risks of foreign investments are typically greater in less developed countries, which are sometimes referred to as emerging markets. For example, legal, political, and economic structures in these countries may be changing rapidly, which can cause instability and greater risk of loss. These countries are also more likely to experience higher levels of inflation, deflation, or currency devaluation, which could hurt their economies and securities markets. For these and other reasons, investments in emerging markets are often considered speculative. Similarly, investors are also subject to foreign securities risks including, but not limited to, the fact that foreign investments may be subject to different and in some circumstances less stringent regulatory and disclosure standards than U.S. investments.

This material represents an assessment of the market environment at a specific point in time and is not intended to be a forecast of future events, or a guarantee of future results. This information is not intended to be individual or personalized investment or tax advice and should not be used for trading purposes. data.title: Touchstone Mid Cap Value Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund Highlights

  • Utilizes a classic value-driven fundamental investment process
  • Seeks to identify companies believed to be selling at a discount to their intrinsic value
  • Employs five valuation screens that seek to identify attractively priced securities
  • Conducts in-depth research and analysis on the securities that pass the valuation screens in an effort to identify leading companies selling at attractive valuations
  • Examines financial statements and assesses the company’s management team, competitive strategy and its current market position

Market Recap

Style factors across our asset class were a headwind to performance. Within the Russell Midcap® Value Index, high volatility stocks notably outperformed more defensive, low volatility positions. There was little correlation across price-to-earnings valuations, though non-earners performed best. Stocks with no yield outperformed those with high dividend yields. Stocks in the highest quintiles of return on equity outperformed those in the lower quintiles.

Within the benchmark, the best performing sectors were Information Technology (IT), Consumer Discretionary, and Utilities. Consumer Staples, Real Estate, Energy, Materials, and Health Care sectors notably underperformed.

Portfolio Review

The Touchstone Mid Cap Value Fund (Class A Shares, Load Waived)(TCVAX) underperformed its benchmark, the Russell Midcap® Value Index, for the quarter ended September 30, 2025.

The Fund’s sector level relative performance was mixed. Consumer Discretionary, Financials, Energy, and Industrials were the largest sector laggards. Relative performance was strongest in Health Care, Utilities, and IT.

The Utilities sector was a modest contributor to performance led by Xcel Energy Inc. (XEL). The regulated utility has been trading at a discount to peers due to questions about liability in the case of a Colorado fire. Late in the quarter, prior to the start of a trial, the company reached a settlement for an amount much smaller than anticipated by the market.

The IT sector contributed to relative performance, led by strong results from Lumentum Holdings Inc. (LITE) and PTC Inc. (PTC). LITE reported strong results and raised 2025 guidance expectations as telecom and datacenter growth for artificial intelligence (AI) and cloud computing continue to boost demand for the company’s optical solutions. Software company PTC reported a strong pipeline of new business, a benefit stemming from a change in their sales organization, and the company was also the subject of takeover speculation. EPAM Systems Inc. (EPAM) was a notable underperformer in the sector. The software engineering firm reported solid results but lagged in a market that was narrowly focused on AI.

The Materials sector was a modest detractor during the quarter, with Ball Corp. (BALL), the worst performing name. The aluminum packaging company declined as beer consumption was weaker than expected, though increased energy and soft drink demand should be enough to offset any earnings weakness. However, Eagle Materials Inc. (EXP) outperformed as pricing for cement and wallboard held up better than expected despite choppy demand patterns for both materials.

Darling Ingredients Inc. (DAR) was the largest detractor in the Consumer Staples sector. The rendering and biofuels company continues to wait for final rules from the Environmental Protection Agency regarding renewable volume obligations and small refinery exemptions, and capping prices for their products over the short term.

The Industrials sector weighed on relative returns in the quarter, despite solid results from several holdings. Industrial distributor WESCO International Inc. (WCC) outperformed given their favorable exposure to data center builds. Two defense companies also outperformed, Leidos Holdings Inc. (LDOS) and L3Harris Technologies Inc. (LHX). Leidos’ multiple has recovered following solid results and the end of DOGE-related fears from earlier in the year. LHX has product offerings that align well with the priorities of the new administration: golden dome, space, and munitions restocking.

The Financials sector underperformed during the quarter. Pinnacle Financial Partners Inc. (PNFP), a bank with a strong franchise in the southeastern United States, announced an unexpected merger with Synovus Financial Corp. (SNV). We expect share losses and a difficult integration, and we exited the position as a result. Reinsurance Group of America Inc. (RGA) lagged in the quarter, as a reversal of positive mortality rates resulted in an earnings miss. Ameriprise Financial Inc. (AMP) experienced headwinds from low customer cash balances, reduced asset flows in their wrap platform, and increased competition for advisor acquisitions. On the positive side, investment bank Evercore Inc. (EVR) benefited from increased advisory fees and tight expense control.

The largest underperforming sector in the Fund was the Consumer Discretionary sector, led by LKQ Corp. (LKQ), an auto salvage and parts company. The company continues to see weak repair volumes as customers have been reluctant to fix damaged vehicles due to rising auto insurance rates. Auto parts manufacturers Gentex Corp. (GNTX) and BorgWarner Inc. (BWA) both outperformed in the quarter on good execution and more clarity around potential tariff related headwinds.

As we enter the fourth quarter, we are notably underweight in the Real Estate and Communication Services sectors. These exposures are driven by the opportunity set we see in each sector. During the quarter we initiated positions in Microchip Technology (MCHP) (IT sector), SouthState Bank Corp. (SSB) (Financials sector), Packaging Corp. (PKG)(Materials sector), and Schlumberger Limited (SLB)(Energy sector). We exited our positions in ChampionX Corp. (Energy sector), Columbia Sportswear Co. (COLM)(Consumer Discretionary sector), NiSource Inc. (NI)(Utility sector), Pinnacle Financial Partners Inc. (Financials sector), and Qorvo Inc. (QRVO)(IT sector).

Outlook and Conclusion

Crosswinds persist as we move into the final quarter of 2025. While a well-telegraphed reduction in interest rates boosted returns in the third quarter, the pace of future rate reductions is still in question, as is the independence of the U.S. Federal Reserve. Inflation continues to run at elevated rates while job creation has stagnated, creating diametrically opposing signals for decision makers. Tariffs, policy changes, and a government shutdown are all creating unevenness across the global economy. The AI boom has been a key driver for markets over the last two years, and a valuation bubble continues to be actively debated among investors.

Amid these market dynamics, we continue to hold fast and invest according to our process. Fundamentally we are looking for quality stocks, trading at a discount, with good risk/reward. We look for companies with strong management teams, high barriers to entry, solid balance sheets, and we continue to rigorously examine downside scenarios for our positions.

We continue to find attractively valued investment opportunities with favorable risk/reward profiles. While we do not believe in making short-term projections, we believe these investments will outperform the market longer term.
